desc: Rye is one of those English short-break towns that looks almost too arranged until you stay long enough to notice the marshes, harbour history and bird-filled edge beyond the postcard streets. Mermaid Street, the Ypres Tower, antique shops and old inns are the obvious draw, but the town works best when paired with Camber Sands or Rye Harbour Nature Reserve rather than treated as a one-street photo stop.

todo.name: Mermaid Street
todo.desc: Walk the cobbled hill early before day visitors arrive and shopfronts get busy.

todo.name: Ypres Tower and town walls
todo.desc: Use the tower and viewpoints to understand Rye's defensive and maritime past.

todo.name: Camber Sands
todo.desc: Add a wind-aware beach session at Camber when tides and weather suit.

todo.name: Rye Harbour Nature Reserve
todo.desc: Walk the flat reserve for birds, shingle coast and big skies.

todo.name: St Mary's tower views
todo.desc: Climb for the marsh, harbour and red-roof perspective that explains Rye's old port geography.

todo.name: Lamb House and citadel lanes
todo.desc: Use the quieter lanes around Lamb House to feel the town beyond the busiest camera spots.

wifi.note: Usually solid in inns, cafes and rentals, but thick historic buildings, reserve walks and beach days make mobile data a useful backup.

prac.fltNote: Rye has no airport and no destination-level LGW/LCY nonstop routing. In practice this is a rail-and-road town: the station is central, staffed and much more useful than trying to think in airport terms, while Camber beach traffic and hot-weather parking pressure often matter more than any inbound flight choice. If you are arriving from London airports, plan the final rail leg rather than assuming the airport distance tells you much.
prac.lang: English; Sussex and Romney Marsh place names are common in local visitor context
prac.visa: UK domestic destination for British citizens; Irish and other travellers should verify UK entry rules if applicable.
alerts: Cobbles, slopes and older inns are awkward with wheeled luggage.
Camber traffic and parking can be difficult on hot weekends.
Some restaurants need reservations despite the town's small size.
Reserve and marsh walks are exposed, so wind and sun can feel stronger than in town.

sweet: May, June and September are the best balance for old-town wandering, reserve walks and beach add-ons without the full squeeze of peak summer weekends.
